e. d. Sanders, of the firm of
Powell Sanders, returned home
from a business trip through the
Walla Walla country. Regarding
conditions among the farmers OF
the Inland Empire Mr.. Sanders said
they were never better. The yield
of grain has been enormous and the
high prices for foodstuffs insures
year of unexampled prosperity in
the rural districts. He says con-
dition in Spokane are normal and
collections were never better. The
closing of the Butte savings bank
will be to an extent bad thing for
this part of the country, but con-
ditions will soon become more set
Tiled and no serious results are ex-
ypected.


1.-THE Inland Empire" said Mr..
Sanders, was never in better
shape to weather a financial storm,
and personally do not believe the
present Wall st. crisis will have
any detrimental effect on the Pa
cific Northwest. In the end, good
will result from the water SqueeZ-
ing process stocks are now passing
through, and a great business re-
vival on sound principles will sure
ly result.


The financial furry will, l am
afraid, have one bad effect on Spo-
kane. | don't believe we will get
better freight rates through ia
vorable decision by the interstate
commerce commission. as further
contraction of receipts by the rail
roads would have the effect of in
creasing the depression of stocks
and for that reason | expect we
will be deprived of much needed
reform in freight rates for this
section"
